Welcome back to What I\xe2\x80\x99m Hearing, and happy M.L.K. Day, college football national championship game day, and Trump Inauguration Day. Given the embarrassing holiday weekend at the box office, let\xe2\x80\x99s just pretend the moviegoing year starts\xe2\x80\xa6 now.

Jan 21, 2025

Matthew Belloni

Welcome back to What I\xe2\x80\x99m Hearing, and happy M.L.K. Day, college football national championship game day, and Trump Inauguration Day. (In that order? I guess it depends on if you\xe2\x80\x99re hoping to merge your large, financially challenged media company.)

Given the embarrassing holiday weekend at the box office, let\xe2\x80\x99s just pretend the moviegoing year starts\xe2\x80\xa6 now . Tomorrow is our annual Box Office Draft episode of The Town (please send me\xe2\x80\x94and not Lucas \xe2\x80\x94your proprietary insights on \xe2\x80\x9925 releases), and tonight our box office Nostradamus Scott Mendelson has his biggest questions (and a few answers) about a fascinating year.

\xf0\x9f\x8e\xa7 Programming note : This week on The Town , CBS president Amy Reisenbach explained her new greenlight calculus, and the Assistants vs. Agents guy, Warner Bailey , told some very fun assistant horror stories. Subscribe here and here .

Got a news tip or an idea for me? Just reply to this email or message me on Signal at 310-804-3198.

Discussed in this issue : Ryan Gosling , Justin Baldoni , Brad Cafarelli , Kevin Hart , Margot Robbie , David Zaslav , Ryan Reynolds , Brady Corbet , Pam Abdy , Mel Gibson , Kathy Kennedy , Jake Paul , John Krasinski , Dave Grohl , and\xe2\x80\xa6 a curious art sale.

But first\xe2\x80\xa6

Who Won the Week: Benson Boone

The best new artist Grammy nominee with the Chalamet -esque mustache scored the number one song of 2024 with \xe2\x80\x9cBeautiful Things,\xe2\x80\x9d per Luminate\xe2\x80\x99s year-end report, topping Sabrina Carpenter \xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9cEspresso\xe2\x80\x9d and Billie Eilish \xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9cBirds of a Feather . \xe2\x80\x9d

Runner-up : Jason Bateman , who\xe2\x80\x99s coming for Millie Bobby Brown \xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9cBiggest Star on Netflix\xe2\x80\x9d title. His preposterous airport thriller, Carry-On , just hopped her Damsel to hit number five on the all-time most watched original movies list.

Honorable mention : Brad Cafarelli , the publicist, whose client Whoopi Goldberg just so happened to vigorously defend another of his clients, Carrie Underwood , against backlash from performing at Trump \xe2\x80\x99s inauguration.

A little more on the year in music : A big \xe2\x80\x9924 for pop acts, indie labels, and international growth, per the full Luminate study. And brands and casting directors got a new \xe2\x80\x9cindex\xe2\x80\x9d for gauging an artist\xe2\x80\x99s personal reach/value. It incorporates their streaming numbers, social media footprint, awareness, public perception, and fan engagement. Together they reveal the \xe2\x80\x9cbreadth and affinity of an artist\xe2\x80\x99s fandom among U.S. consumers.\xe2\x80\x9d Some surprises in the nearly all-female Top 10:

    1. Taylor Swift
    2. Adele (!)
    3. Beyonc\xc3\xa9
    4. Elton John
    5. Rihanna
    6. Eminem (!!)
    7. Shakira (!!!)
    8. Ariana Grande
    9. Lady Gaga
    10. Dolly Parton

Definitely amusing to see Adele so high given she\xe2\x80\x99s barely done any endorsements, doesn\xe2\x80\x99t tour beyond her just-ended Vegas residency, and hasn\xe2\x80\x99t released new music since 2021. Also, where is Snoop ?

The Brutalist Truth About Oscars Controversies

Do we care that The Brutalist used A.I.? So asked a veteran Oscar voter in a text to me after its editor, D\xc3\xa1vid Jancs\xc3\xb3 , said the filmmakers employed the dreaded technology to fix Hungarian dialogue and generate those nifty architecture designs at the end. The interview necessitated weekend damage control by director Brady Corbet , who admitted to using the Respeecher tool but insisted it was \xe2\x80\x9cdone with the utmost respect for the craft.\xe2\x80\x9d Please . Who thinks Corbet made a 215-minute period epic for $10 million without using whatever means necessary to cut costs? A.I. is everywhere now, especially in the postproduction process. Emilia P\xc3\xa9rez apparently also used the Respeecher tool and nobody cared, though many in Mexico seem to wish the filmmakers had fixed Selena Gomez \xe2\x80\x99s accent.

What\xe2\x80\x99s interesting about this Oscars \xe2\x80\x9ccontroversy\xe2\x80\x9d is how little this stuff matters now. For the past decade or so, awards campaigns lived in fear of an attack that would catch fire on social media, bleed into the conversation among voters, and ultimately derail the narrative. But even in 2019, at the height of Twitter, Green Book won best picture amid a constant feed of vitriol online about its white savior stereotypes. Casey Affleck won best actor for Manchester by the Sea in 2017 despite all the noise around his settlement of a sexual harassment lawsuit. Jojo Rabbit was pilloried by the Very Online Left\xe2\x80\x94until it scored six Oscar nominations and won best adapted screenplay.

That stuff has never really mattered to the Academy when they like a movie. And it certainly doesn\xe2\x80\x99t matter now that Twitter has imploded and social media has been delegitimized as a swamp of misinformation and dance videos. In fact, the most important aspect of Andrea Riseborough \xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9cviral\xe2\x80\x9d best actress campaign of 2022 wasn\xe2\x80\x99t the social media love from Oscars \xe2\x80\x9cinfluencers\xe2\x80\x9d like Jamie Lee Curtis or Frances Fisher ; instead it was the personal outreach from the campaign, the starry Q&A hosts and tastemaker screenings, and the televised endorsements from the likes of Cate Blanchett that fed all media. That\xe2\x80\x99s what has always mattered most.

So no, the A.I. in The Brutalist won\xe2\x80\x99t lose it best picture, nor will the cultural missteps in Emilia P\xc3\xa9rez (even that \xe2\x80\x9cpenis to vagina\xe2\x80\x9d song), or the lack of intimacy coordinators on Anora , or whatever other issues coincidentally arise once nominations are announced on Thursday.

Quote of the Week

\xe2\x80\x9cAny chance the position comes with an ambassador\xe2\x80\x99s residence?\xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x94 Mel Gibson , whose house burned in the Palisades fire, responding to what was apparently his surprise appointment as one of Trump\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9cspecial ambassadors to Hollywood.\xe2\x80\x9d

Runner-up : \xe2\x80\x9cIf you ever get around to watching Game of Thrones , you\xe2\x80\x99ll appreciate that I\xe2\x80\x99m Khaleesi, and like her, I happen to have a few dragons.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94 Blake Lively , in an alleged text to Justin Baldoni , seemingly referencing the power of her husband Ryan Reynolds and friend Taylor Swift , as quoted in Baldoni\xe2\x80\x99s 187-page (!) lawsuit against Lively et al .

Honorable mention : \xe2\x80\x9cI can\xe2\x80\x99t believe you\xe2\x80\x99ve done this much fucking research into cowbells.\xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x94 Dave Grohl , in the episode of Peacock\xe2\x80\x99s four-part SNL documentary focused just on the 2000 \xe2\x80\x9cMore Cowbell\xe2\x80\x9d sketch .

Now on to the year ahead at the box office\xe2\x80\xa6

Five Crucial Box Office Questions for \xe2\x80\x9925

With the strikes and Covid a distant (if bitter) memory, Hollywood is primed for a return to the old normal. Maybe? What will it take for this year to be The One?

Scott Mendelson

Two of the three major studio releases of 2025 have already performed as well as could be expected. Indeed, with Den of Thieves 2 notching a $15.5 million opening, followed by Sony\xe2\x80\x99s R-rated comedy One of Them Days earning $13 million over the M.L.K. holiday frame\xe2\x80\x94you could be forgiven for thinking it\xe2\x80\x99s 2010 all over again. But the success of those relatively modest releases can\xe2\x80\x99t compensate for a holiday weekend that was among the worst since the late \xe2\x80\x9990s. This dissonance once again reveals an industry in existential crisis: Overall grosses now must be weighed against how each film performs in relation to the new expectations and financial requirements of this multichannel age.

The Survive \xe2\x80\x99til \xe2\x80\x9925 mantra has landed movies at a crucial crossroads. With the pandemic and strike delays finally gone, this year\xe2\x80\x99s slate will approximate the number of wide theatrical releases that the exhibition industry became accustomed to in the late 2010s. But will that volume lead to 2010s-level box office? Here\xe2\x80\x99s a closer look at five of the biggest themes, anxieties, and fantasies as the year kicks off\xe2\x80\xa6

I. Can the International Box Office Recover?

While the theatrical industry was preoccupied with whether last year\xe2\x80\x99s overall domestic box office could reach 2023\xe2\x80\x99s #Barbenheimer-enhanced $8.9 billion, it should have been more concerned with the overseas marketplace. In 2024, non-domestic revenue fell 10 percent year over year, compared to 3.3 percent in North America.

That decline was driven by a 25 percent drop in China, where economic struggles have impacted local moviegoing and recent U.S. tentpoles underwhelmed, with none topping $500 million. A stronger dollar is partly to blame, but so is a global populace that has grown less reliant on theaters for their access to American pop culture. The Ryan Gosling \xe2\x80\x93 Emily Blunt action-comedy The Fall Guy did $92 million domestic, $181 million worldwide, and ended up with a 51/49 domestic/international split. Meanwhile, the Ryan Reynolds -starring, John Krasinski -directed If netted a 58/42 split, earning $111 million in North America\xe2\x80\x94a terrific result for an original, non-action, kid-targeted flick\xe2\x80\x94but only $192 million worldwide. Likewise, the Daisy Edgar-Jones \xe2\x80\x93 and Glen Powell -led Twisters earned a rousing $270 million in North America, but barely cracked $100 million internationally.

By now, Hollywood has largely written off the Chinese market for most tentpoles, treating a periodic breakout like Alien: Romulus ($351 million worldwide, with $110 million from China) as a pleasant surprise. But the overall non-domestic dip was 10 percent even outside of China, including 17.5 percent in Japan and 10.7 percent in South Korea.

Yes, breakouts like Wonder Woman or Wicked: Part One , where the domestic overperformance is such that only adequate overseas grosses are required, still happen. And globally skewed hits like Mufasa: The Lion King\xe2\x80\x94 which has earned $382 million out of its current $591 million worldwide cume outside of North America\xe2\x80\x94are still possible. But the notion of a nostalgia franchise like Beetlejuice Beetlejuice playing hard only in North America may presage a durable trend. The once-conventional 40/60 split, even for domestic successes, can no longer be taken for granted.

Will this mean that two of this year\xe2\x80\x99s likely global megahits, Jurassic World: Rebirth and Avatar: Fire and Ash , will have to pull more of their weight stateside than previous installments? Perhaps, although those franchises tend to buck the trends by virtue of their uniqueness and generational popularity. Moreover, it\xe2\x80\x99s not like either title will suffer if it earns only 75 percent of its predecessor\xe2\x80\x94or around $750 million and $1.74 billion, respectively. But they are the exceptions, not the rule.

II. Has Marvel Lost Its Luster?

Disney gave the MCU a sabbatical in 2024, slating just one surefire smash\xe2\x80\x94 Deadpool & Wolverine \xe2\x80\x94for the entire year. Part of that film\xe2\x80\x99s success can be attributed to the fact that it featured established franchise characters, beloved even among those unafflicted by a slavish devotion to the MCU. Marvel won\xe2\x80\x99t have that same assurance this year. After all, Captain America: Brave New World , Thunderbolts , and The Fantastic Four: First Steps feature new, or at least less established, characters. Still, Disney is hoping that this trio can pull grosses closer to Phase Two ( Thor: The Dark World , earning $644 million) than Phase One ( Thor , earning $449 million).

But will general audiences care about another Captain America movie\xe2\x80\x94this one headlined by Anthony Mackie \xe2\x80\x94nearly six years after Chris Evans handed off the shield in Avengers: Endgame ? Will Thunderbolts prove to be a must-see MCU summer kickoff despite an ensemble cast populated with B-level characters plucked from B-level MCU movies and Disney+ shows? Meanwhile, The Fantastic Four: The First Steps will be the fourth attempt in just over 30 years to turn Marvel\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9cFirst Family\xe2\x80\x9d into a viable franchise.

While Captain America: Civil War earned $1.15 billion in May of 2016, that picture was sold as a glorified \xe2\x80\x9c Avengers 2.5 .\xe2\x80\x9d The sequel pitted Earth\xe2\x80\x99s mightiest heroes against each other while introducing MCU versions of Spider-Man and Black Panther. This time out, Brave New World will be essentially just a Captain America movie with a non-Steve Rogers Captain America, albeit with Harrison Ford included.

As we\xe2\x80\x99ve seen recently, the MCU isn\xe2\x80\x99t the\xc2\xa0 primary selling point that it once was. Eternals barely cracked $400 million in 2021, and The Marvels got to only $200 million in 2023. That in-universe continuity no longer had enough automatic must-see value to pull grosses in the $675 million to $875 million range\xe2\x80\x94 i.e. , on par with what used to be expected for non- Avengers Marvel movies.

If only established MCU franchises, such as Doctor Strange or Thor , reach upper-level Phase Two grosses, Disney will be in a bind. You can only recapture 2010s glory by casting Robert Downey Jr. as Doctor Doom so many times. And the multiplex industry that once depended on the MCU franchise as an over-indexing buffer will have to hope that other tentpoles can make up the difference, while non-tentpoles overperform here and there.

III. Are Old Franchises the New Comedies?

These days, it often takes familiar, nostalgic packaging to fill theaters. Audiences will show up for a good laugh only if the film is a hybrid action-comedy, horror-comedy, or within an established and often fantastical franchise. (Think Barbie , Inside Out , Despicable Me , Deadpool , Beetlejuice , M3GAN , and Bad Boys .) This year, Hollywood will apply that logic to smaller-scale, less tentpole-sized properties. At least, that\xe2\x80\x99s the optimistic view of how this year\xe2\x80\x99s revivals will pan out.

Disney\xe2\x80\x99s Freaky Friday: Freakier Friday is a pure nostalgia play that targets the grown-ups who made the Lindsay Lohan \xe2\x80\x93 Jamie Lee Curtis remake a hit more than 20 years ago. The same could be said of Paramount\xe2\x80\x99s Naked Gun reboot, starring Liam Neeson and Pamela Anderson . Sony\xe2\x80\x99s comedic Anaconda revamp, starring Jack Black and Paul Rudd , could be another example of a studio scraping the bottom of the barrel for any past hit that could be retrofitted into a franchise reboot. Sony, however, could potentially learn from its blockbuster Jumanji revival, and craft something good enough to appeal to moviegoers who couldn\xe2\x80\x99t care less about the original. Along with Paramount\xe2\x80\x99s planned Scary Movie revival, these remakes could remind audiences that they used to show up to theaters for the exclusive purpose of laughing for 90-120 minutes. (This year also features legacy sequels of Karate Kid and I Know What You Did Last Summer , but neither is a reboot or remake.)

IV. Will F1 Change Apple\xe2\x80\x99s Thin-Skinned Stance on Theaters?

Set for a late June theatrical release, courtesy of Warner Bros., the very pricey Joseph Kosinski -directed Formula One melodrama starring Brad Pitt may be the final test for Apple\xe2\x80\x99s theatrical release strategy. After a string of embarrassing flops like Argylle and Fly Me to the Moon , bad press on F1 could take Apple out of the theatrical game for good.


So the stakes are high. As we saw with Margot Robbie \xe2\x80\x99s Wuthering Heights, which Warner Bros. snatched from Netflix with less money but the promise of theaters, the out-of-home debut remains a powerful carrot. Netflix likely won\xe2\x80\x99t budge beyond limited\xe2\x80\x94or, perhaps, token \xe2\x80\x94theatrical releases. Success for the just-announced, Imax-only, four-week theatrical window for Greta Gerwig \xe2\x80\x99s Narnia over Thanksgiving 2026 is unlikely to turn Ted Sarandos into a multiplex true believer. And my guess is that Apple, despite its borderline fanatical desire to work with major stars and filmmakers, will only open itself up to theatrical if F1 becomes an unquestionable commercial success.

V. Is Zaz Betting Warners on a Retro Strategy?

Despite all the emphasis on James Gunn \xe2\x80\x99s long-awaited Superman , Warners\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9925 theatrical slate is the most ambitious and original of any major studio this year, featuring several big cinematic swings for film chiefs Mike De Luca and Pam Abdy . Is this the result of David Zaslav \xe2\x80\x99s desire to promote Warner Bros. as a big-deal, old-school movie studio, or simply make it (and/or WBD) more attractive to buyers? Perhaps it\xe2\x80\x99s a bit of both.

A reportedly $140 million Paul Thomas Anderson film, even one starring Leo DiCaprio, sounds like commercial madness, particularly after the lopsided buzz-to-box office performance of Licorice Pizza. Indeed, There Will Be Blood is P.T.A.\xe2\x80\x99s top grosser, with just $76 million worldwide. But DiCaprio is among the last butts-in-seats, bona fide movie stars, so if ever there were a time to place a bet on the auteur, this might be it.

Warners will also release Bong Joon Ho \xe2\x80\x99s Mickey 17 , a reportedly $150 million sci-fi comedy starring Robert Pattinson ; Barry Levinson \xe2\x80\x99s mob drama The Alto Knights, starring two Robert De Niros ; and Ryan Coogler \xe2\x80\x99s vampire movie, Sinners, starring Michael B. Jordan as twin brothers, potentially a future franchise. In the key early October slot, where WB launched Joker , Gravity , and A Star Is Born , the studio is releasing The Bride! , a Bride of Frankenstein riff written and directed by Maggie Gyllenhaal , starring Jessie Buckley and Christian Bale .

Yes, Warners still has franchise flicks like A Minecraft Movie, The Conjuring: Last Rites, and Mortal Kombat 2 . But the De Luca-Abdy non -franchise lineup suggests an alternate, retro universe, where audiences still show up for original movies because of the concepts, well-liked stars, and filmmakers. Audiences certainly claim they want those movies\xe2\x80\xa6 If they actually do, Warner Bros. could emerge from 2025 as more than just the home of Batman and Harry Potter, and maybe give Zaz a reason to fetch his tux from the dry cleaner.

My Reading List\xe2\x80\xa6

Peter Hamby explains how Trump\xe2\x80\x99s billionaire courtship could go wrong\xe2\x80\x94for everyone. [ Puck ]

Devo\xe2\x80\x99s Mark Mothersbaugh makes about $1 million a year from his writing credit on the Ridiculousness theme song. [ Rolling Stone ]

Matthew Ball \xe2\x80\x99s annual state of video gaming presentation reveals how much TikTok and other social platforms have cut into playing time. [ Matthew Ball ]

We get it, guys. The two new Warner Bros. Discovery board members, IAC\xe2\x80\x99s exiting C.E.O. Joey Levin and SoFi head Anthony Noto , bring \xe2\x80\x9ctransaction experience\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9cover 20 years of mergers and acquisitions\xe2\x80\x9d to the company that may or may not (definitely may!) be looking for a merger of its own. [ PR Newswire ]

Add Kevin Hart \xe2\x80\x99s Hartbeat to the list of talent-fronted production outfits that aren\xe2\x80\x99t justifying their ridiculous valuations from the Peak TV boom. [ Bloomberg ]

Maverick Carter , whose SpringHill Entertainment is already losing millions, wants to raise $5 billion to take on the NBA. Joe Pompliano is rightfully skeptical. [ Huddle Up ]

Lauren Sherman looks at how the L.A. fires are impacting the red carpet side of awards season and beyond. [ Puck ]

Hmmmm : Kathy Kennedy and Frank Marshall , who sold their Malibu house last year, also recently offloaded a big chunk of their art collection. [ Puck ]

The Feedback

Good responses to my Thursday report on Greta Gerwig \xe2\x80\x99s deal with Imax and Netflix for \xe2\x80\x98Narnia\xe2\x80\x99 and my ideas for Hollywood\xe2\x80\x99s role in the L.A. fire rebuild. Some examples\xe2\x80\xa6

\xe2\x80\x9cGood for Greta, but the real shame is that a four-week [theatrical] window is now considered a \xe2\x80\x98win.\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94 A filmmaker

\xe2\x80\x9cShe thinks she\xe2\x80\x99s getting an actual theatrical release\xe2\x80\xa6 not remotely quite. This is just a platform release on steroids. Wait till she finds out they won\xe2\x80\x99t be reporting box office.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94 An executive

\xe2\x80\x9cAbsolutely agree on committing to more production in L.A., but the incentive structure should be focused on volume and not just a few big productions. California should structure it similar to the U.K. incentive where they support big productions, but also streamline indie film applications and money up to a certain budget threshold (like $20 million). Additionally, L.A. should create some type of regional credit for small films (tier 1) so those little films stop jetting off to Louisville or Oklahoma. Those are the bedrock productions of a talent pipeline in our town and industry.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94 An executive

\xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99d be curious to hear more about how Brady Corbet delivers a period epic for $10 million, and yet L.A.-shot Joker 2 cost 20 times that. The industry is going to have to figure out how to deliver quality content at scale and at cost\xe2\x80\x94maybe L.A. can help lead the way in that regard, given its concentration of ambitious, emerging talent and empty soundstages. Wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t that be the right direction for L.A.\xe2\x80\x94offer serious, low-cost alternatives to development and production that would set the stage for the future of the storytelling industry?\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94 A producer

\xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m proposing a streamlined rehousing fund to provide immediate relief to displaced crew and non-union workers. While the industry unions and organizations like The Motion Picture & Television Fund are stepping in, this initiative would fast-track rehousing efforts, addressing an urgent need for those who keep our industry running. Long term, this fund, with investment from larger industry players, could be used to build affordable housing for future industry professionals, ensuring Los Angeles remains a global hub for skilled crews and innovative storytelling. This isn\xe2\x80\x99t just about providing shelter, it\xe2\x80\x99s about securing the future of Hollywood by supporting the people who\xe2\x80\x99ve always made it possible.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94 A screenwriter

Finally\xe2\x80\xa6 Chart of the Week

Ahead of Netflix\xe2\x80\x99s earnings tomorrow, Antenna is reporting 656,000 sign-ups to the service over the three-day period surrounding its exclusive NFL games on Christmas Day. A great subscription event, for sure, but no match for the Jake Paul - Mike Tyson boxing stunt a month earlier\xe2\x80\xa6

Have a great week,

Matt

Got a question, comment, complaint, or want to donate to fire relief? Click here or email me at Matt@puck.news or call/text me at 310-804-3198.
